title: /dummyindex skill arg-routing can't handle the CLI's own `usage` verb — `/dummyindex usage chat` treated as a bogus path scope, resolved only via user round-trip
evidence: [assistant:] "`/dummyindex` treats its arguments as a **scope path** to index. \"usage chat\" isn't a path, and it's not a recognized flag" ... [AskUserQuestion:] "`/dummyindex usage chat` doesn't map to a valid index scope (no `usage chat` path) or a dummyindex flag. What did you intend?" ... [tokens skill output:] "For history across all projects, mention `dummyindex usage daily | session | monthly | blocks`."
what_happened: The user typed `/dummyindex usage chat` wanting token-usage stats. The SKILL.md instructs treating tokens after /dummyindex strictly as a literal scope path (or fail as 'ambiguous scope'), even though the CLI itself ships a `usage` subcommand family. Claude correctly avoided launching an expensive full ingest but had to probe the filesystem and ask the user a clarifying question before redirecting to /tokens. A dangerous near-miss: a less careful reading of the skill would have kicked off a full council re-index over the repo.
root_cause_guess: SKILL.md scope-resolution rules predate / ignore the CLI's non-indexing verbs; the skill should recognize known CLI subcommands (usage, install, context ...) in args before path interpretation.

title: No discoverable read-only status entry point — Claude guessed `dummyindex --status` and got 'error: unknown command'
evidence:
build status [mcp-server]: 42/42 done
error: unknown command '--status'
Usage: dummyindex <command> [args]

Commands:
  install [--scope user|project] [--dir PATH] [--skill-only] ...
what_happened: While disambiguating `/dummyindex usage chat`, Claude wanted a read-only overview of the index state and ran `dummyindex --status` (with `dummyindex context status` as fallback) — the flag doesn't exist and the CLI dumped its full usage text. Only `context build --proposal X --status` exists, which requires knowing a proposal slug. Single occurrence.
root_cause_guess: There is no top-level `status`/`context status` verb summarizing index freshness/equipment/proposals, so the model improvises plausible-but-wrong flags. Error message also doesn't suggest the nearest valid command.

title: equip apply summary miscounts and under-reports: 'wrote 6 item(s)' while listing only 4 writes; adopt + hook lines silently dropped from apply output
evidence:
equip: stack=python frameworks=['FastAPI']
  write   python-implementer  ->  .claude/agents/python-implementer.md
  write   python-tester  ->  .claude/agents/python-tester.md
  write   backend-reviewer  ->  .claude/agents/backend-reviewer.md
  write   backend-verify  ->  .claude/skills/backend-verify/SKILL.md
equip: wrote 6 item(s), skipped 0, preserved 0 user-modified, kept 0 evolved
what_happened: The dry-run plan showed 6 actions (4 writes + 1 registry adopt + 1 settings.json hook) but the apply output printed only the 4 file writes yet claimed 'wrote 6 item(s)' — the manifest-only adoption and the settings.json hook wiring were counted as 'written' but not echoed. Claude had to run a separate python inspection of equipment.json and settings.json to confirm what actually got wired (it had: DUMMYINDEX_EQUIP and DUMMYINDEX_AUTO_REFRESH both present, so wiring itself was correct).
root_cause_guess: Apply-path logging only prints file-render actions while the counter includes all manifest entries (adopts, hooks); dry-run and apply renderers diverge.

title: CLI --check mutates checklist.md underneath the session, causing an Edit conflict
evidence: Error: File has been modified since read, either by the user or by a linter. Read it again before attempting to write it. [while editing .context/proposals/mcp-server/checklist.md immediately after running `dummyindex context build --proposal mcp-server --check 7`]
what_happened: Claude alternates between `dummyindex context build --check N` (CLI rewrites checklist.md to tick the box) and direct Edits to the same file (rewording items). Once, the CLI's mutation invalidated Claude's read-state and the Edit was rejected, forcing a re-read. One occurrence, recovered immediately, but it is an inherent shared-mutable-file hazard of the design (the index-based --check addressing also silently depends on item order that Claude's own edits could shift).
root_cause_guess: Build state lives only in the human/agent-edited markdown checklist; the CLI offers no advisory output mode or stable item IDs, so concurrent CLI and editor mutation of the same file races.

title: Stale 0.15.0 dummyindex deployed in repo; version check only detects CLI/skill mismatch, not staleness
evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.15.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`. ... [tool_result] 0.15.0 | --- | context propose: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.context/proposals/brand-centric-migration (4 files)
what_happened: The repo's .claude/skills/dummyindex-plan and the CLI (installed inside the project's own .venv — `./.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/dummyindex/cli/bootstrap.py` shows up in repo file searches) were both pinned at 0.15.0 while upstream dummyindex had moved several minor versions ahead (equip, plugin manager, critique panel, wave checklists, reconcile loop). The skill's only guard is 'confirm the CLI matches' — both layers were equally stale so the check passed and the session ran the old behavior: flat checklist (serial build), no critique panel, no equip/equipment.json, closing step is `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` rather than the reconcile procedure.
root_cause_guess: Per-repo skill copies + a per-project venv CLI install mean a frozen snapshot with no freshness signal; the version-match check can never detect 'both stale'. Needs an upstream-latest check / update nudge (what /dummyindex-update later became) and ideally not vendoring the CLI into the project venv.

title: Proposal cites symbols.json grep line numbers as if they were source-file locations
evidence: Grounding cmd output: "=== key symbols (path:line) === | 358: \"name\": \"_build_owner_filter\", | ... | 5870: \"name\": \"ContainerService\"," → plan.md: "6. **Remove containers.** Delete `app/core/workspaces/containers.py` (`ContainerService` :5870)" and summary: "`ContainerService` (`workspaces/containers.py:5870`, delete), `OwnerRef`/`owner_fks` (`resources/models.py:5272/5298`...)"
what_happened: Following the skill instruction to 'cite reused symbols from .context/map/symbols.json', Claude grepped the monolithic symbols.json and harvested grep's line numbers (358, 956, 969, 5272, 5298, 5870, 6000 — offsets inside the JSON file) then wrote them into spec.md/plan.md as if they were source-file line numbers (containers.py is nowhere near 5870 lines). Build agents executing this checklist would chase bogus locations. Repeated across ~8 symbol citations in the written proposal.
root_cause_guess: symbols.json is one giant JSON blob and the skill gives no retrieval verb (e.g. `dummyindex query symbol <name>` returning path:line), so the model improvises grep and confuses JSON offsets with source locations. Provide a symbol-lookup command or embed path:line next to each name in the docs the skill tells the model to read.

title: Deterministic backbone over-segmented 1022 files into 734 communities; documented council would cost ~1000+ agent dispatches
evidence: The deterministic backbone shattered this 1022-file repo into **734 Leiden communities** (650 trivial, 84 "non-trivial" ...). Yet the actual product structure is only **6 feature slices** ... Following the documented council *strictly* means ~84 per-feature councils + **650 individual trivial-consolidation agent passes** ... ≈ **1000+ agent dispatches** — wildly out of proportion with the "standard ≈ $6–10" the onboarding implied (that estimate assumed N≈14, not 734).
what_happened: On this Next.js frontend the graph-community detection produced 734 features for what is really a ~13-feature repo. The documented council pipeline scaled with raw community count, contradicting the cost estimate shown at onboarding. Claude had to stop and ask; the user instructed 'be smart about ti first do a llm council pass and reduce the segments before moving into actual enrichment', and Claude improvised an off-script taxonomy collapse (ad-hoc bucketing scripts, an architect agent, scaffold/remove surgery).
root_cause_guess: Leiden community detection with no post-clustering consolidation pass is unsuited to feature-sliced frontend repos (many small import islands); the council docs and cost model assume N≈14 features and provide no built-in 'reduce segments first' stage.

title: No batch verbs for taxonomy surgery — 734 sequential features-remove calls took a multi-minute background loop
evidence:
=== features-merge ===
error: --from <id> and --into <id> are both required ...
=== features-remove ===
error: --feature <id> is required
... "removing 734 community features..." [background loop] ... "all communities removed at iter 46" (polling at 6s intervals)
what_happened: Every taxonomy verb (features-merge, features-rename, assign-files, scaffold-feature, features-remove, mark-enriched) accepts exactly one item per invocation, and each call regenerates INDEX.md + graph.json + graph.html. Collapsing 734 communities required a Python subprocess loop run in the background, with Claude polling for ~5 minutes across two wait loops before it finished. mark-enriched likewise required a 13-iteration shell loop.
root_cause_guess: CLI verbs are single-target with per-call full index regeneration (O(n^2) overall); no --from-json/batch mode or deferred-reindex flag exists for bulk restructuring, which the over-segmentation issue makes routine.

title: Subcommands reject --help and there is no status verb; usage only discoverable by provoking errors
evidence: error: unknown command '--status' ... error: unknown argument(s) for `conventions-write`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `equip discover`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `build`: ['--help']
what_happened: At least 4 distinct occurrences: `dummyindex --status` (no such command), and `--help` rejected by conventions-write, equip discover, and build. Each time Claude had to run the verb bare to harvest the 'required args' error, or grep the top-level help, burning turns. The orchestrator skill repeatedly guesses flags the CLI doesn't have.
root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argparse-style dispatcher without per-subcommand --help support or a status command; SKILL.md doesn't embed full per-verb usage, so the model probes interactively.

title: Throwaway scaffold-feature test feature leaked back into the index after non-force removal failed
evidence: error: feature '_zztest' still owns 2 file(s) that exist on disk (e.g. shared/lib/api/fetc... [later] The `_zztest` throwaway leaked back in (my earlier non-force cleanup failed). Let me force-remove it to land on exactly 13 features
what_happened: Claude scaffolded a `_zztest` feature to learn the verb's behavior. Plain features-remove refused because the feature 'owns files that exist on disk' — true of essentially every real feature — so the cleanup silently failed and `_zztest` resurfaced in features/INDEX.json after the big restructure, requiring a second --force removal. The index briefly read 14 features instead of 13.
root_cause_guess: features-remove's no-force guard (refuse if owned files exist on disk) is inverted for the common case; failure exit didn't make the earlier compound command abort, so stale state survived into refresh-indexes.

title: Equip skill ships duplicate .tmpl template copies into the repo that the renderer never reads
evidence: Good news — the dry-run rendered fine, so the CLI bundles its own templates (the `.claude/skills/.../templates/*.tmpl` I deleted were distribution copies, not what the renderer reads).
what_happened: The user asked to delete the 9 .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/*.md.tmpl files. Claude warned this would disable /dummyindex-equip until reinstall — then discovered equip rendered fine anyway because the CLI bundles its own templates. The repo copies are dead weight that (a) misled the assistant about blast radius, and (b) were among the unassigned files that blocked reconcile-stamp.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex install copies equip templates into every repo's .claude/skills tree as documentation/distribution artifacts even though rendering uses package-internal templates; nothing marks them as inert.

title: Initial install built .context/ but never persisted onboarding config, forcing onboarding on the next run
evidence: No `config.json` yet — onboarding never persisted, even though `.context/` exists. ... Onboarding hasn't run, so I need your preferences before the council proceeds. One note on scale: there are **734 raw graph-community detections** right now
what_happened: When /dummyindex ran, .context/ already existed (meta.json created the same day at install time, 1022 files indexed at HEAD) but features were all raw community-N stubs with null summaries and there was no .context/config.json — the install-time project init built the deterministic backbone without running or persisting onboarding, leaving the repo in a half-initialized state the skill had to detect and repair. The onboarding cost estimate it then showed ('standard ≈ $6–10') was wrong for the actual 734-community state.
root_cause_guess: `dummyindex install` project-init builds the backbone but defers onboarding to the skill's first interactive run without flagging the half-built state; the cost copy in onboarding assumes a typical N≈14 feature count rather than reading the actual index.

title: reality-check --demote has no inverse: restoring confidence after false positives required hand-editing feature.json and INDEX.json
evidence: "All 11 features now fully verified (193 claims checked, 0 contradicted). Restoring `INFERRED` on the 8 features the earlier false positives demoted" ... python script: "# Restore INFERRED only where the latest reality-check is clean ... fj['confidence'] = 'INFERRED' ... # Reconcile INDEX.json"
what_happened: The false-positive contradictions (see previous issue) caused `--demote` to flip 8 features from INFERRED to AMBIGUOUS. After the citations were fixed and re-checks came back clean, the CLI offered no 'promote'/'restore' verb, so the orchestrator wrote a raw python loop mutating each feature.json AND .context/features/INDEX.json by hand to undo the demotion — exactly the kind of manual JSON surgery the atomic-verb design (features-rename, council-log) is supposed to prevent.
root_cause_guess: Asymmetric CLI surface: demotion is automated but promotion/recovery is not; a clean re-run of reality-check should auto-restore (or a `--restore`/promote verb should exist).

title: Flow auto-detection is mostly noise: 60 of 75 detected flows discarded (enum-class roots, private helpers, BFS over-reach)
evidence: "workspace-org-core: kept 0, discarded 6. ... **flow-002** (`OrganizationMemberRole`): enum class entry point, 324 steps across 50 files — same pattern" ... "**flow-038** (`ExecStatus`): Enum class entry point (not callable); trace exploded to 100+ steps across 37 unrelated files (breadth mis-" ... "**flow-066** (`build_version_snapshot`): Misdetected breadth — the trace is a reverse call-graph showing which asset/doc service methods call this utility, not a forward execution sequence." Final report: "Flows: 15 kept, 60 discarded (enum-class roots, `_`-private helpers, BFS over-reach)"
what_happened: The deterministic flow extractor treated enum classes and `_`-prefixed private helpers as entry points and produced traces of 100-324 steps across 37-50 unrelated files; at least one trace was a reverse call-graph rather than a forward execution path. Multiple features (ai-chat-streaming, workspace-org-core, app-infra-tools) discarded ALL 6 of their flows. 11 flow-refinement subagent dispatches were spent largely refuting deterministic noise (80% discard rate).
root_cause_guess: Entry-point heuristic doesn't filter non-callable symbols (Enum classes) or private names, and the trace builder does unbounded BFS over the call graph (sometimes following reverse edges) with no breadth/depth cutoff.

title: Entry-point detection misses real FastAPI HTTP handlers, leaving 5 of 11 features with zero flows
evidence: "Caveat: 5 of 11 features kept **0** flows because AST entry-point detection missed the real HTTP handlers (`chat()`, `accept_invitation()`, …) — behaviour for those lives in their `spec.md`/`plan.md`, not `flows/`"
what_happened: The flip side of the flow-noise problem: while inventing flows from enums, the AST extractor failed to detect the decorated FastAPI route handlers that are the actual entry points of this backend, so nearly half the features shipped with no flow narratives at all — a permanent coverage gap in the committed index acknowledged in the final report.
root_cause_guess: Entry-point detection doesn't recognize framework decorators (@router.get/post etc.) as roots; it likely keys off naming/call-graph in-degree only, which misses decorator-registered handlers.

title: Repo's canonical docs in .context/docs/ self-exclude from the source-docs catalog, even when passed explicitly via --docs
evidence: [after `dummyindex ingest ... --docs .../backend/.context/docs --docs .../BOS-Mono/.context/docs`] "backend canon docs: 0" ... Final report: "Note: the backend's own `.context/docs/` canon self-excludes from the catalog — I compensated by injecting explicit canon pointers (CONVENTIONS/DECISIONS/SCHEMA) into every dispatch, which worked"
what_happened: The team keeps its canon (ARCHITECTURE.md, CONVENTIONS.md, DECISIONS.md, SCHEMA.md, BACKLOG.md) in backend/.context/docs/. First ingest auto-discovered only 2 docs (CLAUDE.md, README.md). Re-ingest with the directory passed explicitly via --docs still catalogued 0 backend canon docs (the mono-root's 246 docs were catalogued fine) because the scanner blanket-excludes .context/ as its own output dir. The orchestrator had to hand-inject canon pointers into all ~56 subagent prompts — grounding that future sessions navigating only via the index will NOT get.
root_cause_guess: The ingest doc scanner excludes the .context/ directory wholesale (to avoid indexing its own output) and the exclusion is applied even to explicitly user-supplied --docs roots that happen to live under a .context/ path.

title: `dummyindex ingest --help` fails with an error that names the wrong subcommand
evidence:
$ dummyindex ingest --help
error: unknown argument(s) for `init`: ['--help']
what_happened: While trying to confirm the --docs flag semantics mid-run, the orchestrator asked for subcommand help and got an argparse-style error instead — and the error message refers to `init` (the internal alias target) rather than `ingest` (what was typed). It had to fall back to the top-level `dummyindex --help` wall of text.
root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled arg parsing without per-subcommand --help support; the ingest→`context init` aliasing leaks the internal command name into the error message.

title: Graph partitioner leaks a parser-artifact rationale node into its own single-node community (spurious feature)
evidence: "**Outcome: C — keep as standalone with parser-artifact note. Builder-bug leak recorded.** Evidence: `config_rationale_169` is a `file_type: rationale` node (the `_split_origins` docstring at `app/config.py` L169). The graph partitioner placed it alone into community 14 while every real `config.py` symbol ... landed in communities 1 and 4 ... The node has no edges, no call sites, and no entry points: it was never a real feature member. The `_is_parser_artifact` filter should have dropped `file_type: rationale` nodes before community assignment; one leaked through."
what_happened: Leiden community detection produced a junk feature (`utils-config-fragment`) consisting of a single orphaned docstring-rationale node. It survived the trivial filter into the council, costing a dedicated architect consolidation dispatch (~36k subagent tokens) just to diagnose it as a builder bug, and ends up as a permanent parser-artifact stub feature in the committed index.
root_cause_guess: The `_is_parser_artifact` pre-filter runs incompletely or after edge construction; `file_type: rationale` nodes with zero edges aren't dropped before community assignment, so an isolated node forms its own community.

title: Ephemeral _enrich_plan.json left in .context/ and not gitignored — commit friction and user confusion
evidence: USER: ".context/_enrich_plan.json why wasnt this committed?" ... .context/.gitignore generated by dummyindex covers only: "cache/" and "_doc_backups/" ... assistant: "If you'd rather the enrich plan never reappear as untracked noise, I can add it to `.context/.gitignore` — though dummyindex regenerates that file, so the entry might get overwritten"
what_happened: `dummyindex context enrich-plan` writes a 384KB scratch work-queue into .context/ but the generated .context/.gitignore doesn't cover it. `git add .context` staged it with everything else; the assistant judged it scratch and unstaged it, leaving it as untracked clutter that prompted a user question and a two-turn explanation. Worse, the obvious fix (add it to .gitignore) is unsafe because dummyindex regenerates that .gitignore and may clobber manual entries.
root_cause_guess: enrich-plan's output path was added after the .gitignore template was written; ephemeral artifacts aren't consistently namespaced (e.g. under cache/) or listed in the generated ignore file, and the generated .gitignore has no merge/patch seam for user entries.

title: Onboarding runs after the first full ingest, so external doc roots require a second full re-ingest
evidence: [first ingest] "context init: wrote 218 files ... files: 151" → [source-docs after it] "total docs catalogued: 2\n- CLAUDE.md ... - README.md" → [after onboarding answers] "dummyindex ingest /mnt/.../backend --docs .../backend/.context/docs --docs .../BOS-Mono/.context/docs" → "AST extraction: 151/151 files (100%) | context init: wrote 218 files"
what_happened: The skill's pipeline ingests first, then asks the onboarding questions — including 'any prose-doc roots outside this repo?'. The user's answer (mono-root docs) was persisted to config.json via `context onboard`, but applying it meant re-running the entire ingest with explicit --docs flags (full 151-file AST re-extraction and 218-file rewrite). ingest apparently doesn't read external_docs from the just-written config.json either, since the flags had to be passed manually.
root_cause_guess: Phase ordering in the skill (ingest before onboarding) plus ingest not consuming .context/config.json's external_docs; doc cataloguing isn't an incremental step separable from the full ingest.

title: Resumption state (per-feature source hashes) must be hand-rolled in python; orchestrator unsure of its own file-list schema
evidence:
"Reconciled. Writing the per-feature source hashes (resumption contract per `19-resume.md`), then a final state check:" ... python3 - <<'EOF' ... files = d.get('files') or []
    # files may be list of dicts or strings
    paths = []
what_happened: The skill's resumption contract (19-resume.md) requires per-feature source hashes so re-runs can skip unchanged features, but no CLI verb writes them; the orchestrator implemented hashlib loops over feature.json by hand, including a defensive comment betraying uncertainty about whether `files` holds dicts or strings. Hand-written stamps risk diverging from whatever format the resume logic expects.
root_cause_guess: Missing CLI verb (e.g. `context stamp-sources`) for the resumption contract, combined with an underspecified feature.json files schema in the skill docs.

title: Bare `dummyindex install` (run to elicit usage text) executed a full install and started AST-scanning the entire 1426-file monorepo from the wrong root
evidence:
=== install usage (trigger usage text) ===
  memory skill     ->  /home/ahmed/.claude/skills/dummyindex-remember/SKILL.md
  ...
  skill installed  ->  /home/ahmed/.claude/skills/dummyindex/SKILL.md
  AST extraction: 100/1426 files (7%)
  AST extraction: 1000/1426 files (70%)
[next turn] "That was a mistake — bare `dummyindex install` doesn't print help, it **executed a full install**: it re-registered user-scope skills and began an AST scan of the entire monorepo (1426 files) from the mono root."
what_happened: After `dummyindex install --help` errored, Claude ran bare `dummyindex install` expecting usage text. Instead it immediately performed a full install: re-registered all user-scope skills and began AST extraction of the whole BOS-Mono monorepo (1426 files) from the mono root (not the backend submodule), creating a stray untracked `.context/cache/` at the mono root that Claude then had to verify and `rm -rf`. Damage was limited only because the scan didn't finish writing meta.json/features/.
root_cause_guess: `install` has no `--help` handler and no zero-arg usage/confirmation guard; it treats a bare invocation as 'install here now' and defaults its index root to cwd, which in a monorepo is the superproject rather than the intended submodule.

title: No subcommand `--help` anywhere: reconcile, council-batch, and install all reject `--help` as an unknown argument
evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `reconcile`: ['--help']
...
===council-batch help===
error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help']
...
=== dummyindex install --help ===
error: unknown install argument '--help'
what_happened: Three separate times in one session, asking a subcommand for help produced an argparse-style error instead of usage text. Claude had to fall back to dumping the full `dummyindex context` usage blob and grepping it. Worst consequence: the `install --help` failure led directly to running bare `dummyindex install`, which executed a real install (see installer issue).
root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argument parsing per subcommand without a standard `--help` flag; unknown-argument errors instead of help output. Make `--help` (and bare invocation for destructive verbs) print usage.

title: Five dummyindex installs at four different versions; repo skill wiring silently drifts releases behind the CLI
evidence: "Three installs exist: backend `.venv` (0.24.0), the global `~/.local/bin/dummyindex`, and user-scope skill `~/.claude` (0.24.0) — plus the two repo skills (backend 0.22.0, frontend 0.19.2)." ... "=== which + version === /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex | 0.24.0"
what_happened: The user had to explicitly demand 'both dummyindex need to be on 0.25.0'. Claude found a fragmented install matrix: backend venv dev-dep CLI shadowing a global pip --user CLI, plus three skill copies at 0.24.0/0.22.0/0.19.2. Upgrading required a manual multi-step plan (pip --user upgrade, pyproject floor bump + uv lock/sync, then three `install --skill-only` runs) instead of one update command. The dedicated /dummyindex-update skill was never used — its documented detection covers uv tool/pipx/pip --user but not a project-venv dev-dep install.
root_cause_guess: No single source of truth or self-sync between CLI and the per-repo/per-user skill copies; repo skills only refresh when someone re-runs install. Version-skew detection exists only as a note in SKILL.md, and the update skill's install-detection misses the venv dev-dep pattern.

title: tree.json never enriched: all 1117 nodes still deterministic stubs from the original ingest
evidence:
context enrich-plan: wrote .context/cache/_enrich_plan.json
  total nodes: 1117  stubs: 1117  by_kind: {'project': 1, 'dir': 48, 'file': 176, 'class': 49, 'method': 257, 'function': 586}
  batches: 177
[assistant]: "All 1117 tree nodes are stubs — `tree.json` was never enriched in the original run. That's a **whole-repo, pre-existing gap** (177 file-batches)"
what_happened: During the reconcile, Claude discovered the PageIndex-style tree.json — the artifact future sessions navigate by — was 100% deterministic stubs (1117/1117 nodes), meaning the original full ingest never completed (or never ran) Phase 4.5 tree enrichment. It was flagged as out-of-scope and left unfixed, so retrieval over the tree still reads auto-generated stubs, not prose.
root_cause_guess: The original ingest either skipped tree-enrich silently or was interrupted with no completion check; nothing in status/preflight/drift reporting surfaces 'tree enrichment 0% complete', so the gap persisted invisibly across versions 0.15→0.25.

title: dummyindex install (0.25.0) rewrote .claude/settings.json and silently dropped a user-added Stop hook
evidence: "Heads-up — the 0.25.0 `dummyindex install` **rewrote `settings.json` and dropped my `DUMMYINDEX_BACKBONE_REFRESH` Stop hook** (the canonical install only re-adds memory-nudge + reconcile-gate). That removed the session-end backbone refresh you asked for."
what_happened: When the CLI moved 0.24.0→0.25.0 mid-session and re-ran install, it regenerated .claude/settings.json to the canonical hook set, deleting the custom Stop hook the user had explicitly asked to be wired this same session. The agent had to detect the loss by diffing and hand-restore it (then later removed it anyway because of the rebuild regression).
root_cause_guess: install writes its canonical settings.json hook list non-additively — it replaces the hooks arrays instead of merging/preserving entries that don't carry its own sentinel comments, violating the 'never clobbering a user edit' contract.

title: CLI subcommands reject --help (and other discovery flags) with 'unknown argument(s)'
evidence: "===== council-batch help ===== error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help'] ===== brand-hub council-log ===== error: unknown argument(s) for `council-log`: ['--show']" ... "=== mark-enriched help === error: unknown argument(s) for `mark-enriched`: ['--help'] === section-write help === error: unknown argument(s) for `section-write`: ['--help']" ... "=== equip subcommand help === error: unknown argument(s) for `equip`: ['--help']"
what_happened: Six separate help/discovery attempts failed: council-batch --help, council-log --show, mark-enriched --help, section-write --help, equip --help, plus `dummyindex --status` ("error: unknown command '--status'" — a skill-doc concept that isn't a CLI flag). Each failure forced the agent to grep the giant monolithic `dummyindex context --help` output instead, slowing every decision point.
root_cause_guess: The argparse setup only implements --help at the top/context level; subcommand parsers treat unknown flags as errors rather than supporting per-subcommand help, and the skill docs reference verbs/flags (--status, --show) that don't exist in the CLI.

title: reconcile-stamp --force warning is wrong/misleading about re-reporting
evidence: "context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 29d029b36ab4   WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it: unassigned new files: .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/db-specialist-agent.md.tmpl, ... === verify unassigned cleared === anchor: 29d029b36ab4 | has_drift: False | unassigned: 0"
what_happened: Three reconcile-stamp --force runs each emitted the scary 'WILL be re-reported next reconcile' warning, yet the very next `reconcile --json` showed has_drift: False / unassigned: 0 — files committed behind the anchor drop out, exactly as the user's memory notes predicted. The contradiction sent the agent down extra verification loops each time ("the --force warning was generic") before trusting the actual state.
root_cause_guess: The warning text is emitted unconditionally on --force without checking whether the listed files are committed at/behind the new anchor (in which case they will NOT re-report).

title: Version stamps disagree across layers; mid-session CLI upgrade churned tracked skill files
evidence: "=== CLI on PATH === 0.25.0 === repo skill stamp === 0.25.0 === .context index stamp (meta.json) === dummyindex_version: 0.19.2 | schema_version: 1" ... "Full picture: the **CLI upgraded 0.24.0 → 0.25.0 mid-session** and rewrote the skill files — that's all 11 \"unplaced\" files plus the modified `SKILL.md`/council docs."
what_happened: When the user asked "whats the dummyindex version in this repo?", three stamps disagreed: CLI 0.25.0, repo skill 0.25.0, index meta.json 0.19.2 (and earlier in the same session the skill header said 0.24.0). The mid-session 0.24.0→0.25.0 upgrade rewrote 11 new + ~12 modified tracked .claude/skills files, forcing an unplanned 'sync dummyindex skill to 0.25.0' commit, triggering the reconcile gate, and the stale 0.19.2 index stamp was initially (wrongly) blamed for the re-shatter regression. The index stamp only reached 0.25.0 as an incidental side effect of reconcile-stamp during incident recovery.
root_cause_guess: No single-source version contract: rebuild doesn't update meta.json's dummyindex_version, installs rewrite per-repo skill copies without coordinating a commit, and nothing reconciles the three stamps, so diagnosing version-dependent behavior (like the re-shatter) is guesswork.

title: CLI subcommands reject --help: per-subcommand help is unsupported
evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `assign-files`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help']
=== enrich-plan ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `enrich-plan`: ['--help']
what_happened: Three times during the reconcile close-out Claude tried `dummyindex context <verb> --help` to learn flags (assign-files, council-batch, enrich-plan) and got an argparse-style rejection each time. It fell back to grepping the monolithic top-level help output, slowing discovery of the right reconcile verbs.
root_cause_guess: The custom argument parser treats --help as a positional/unknown arg on subcommands instead of wiring per-subcommand help.

title: council-batch output leaks Python enum repr 'SubagentType.AI' as the agent name
evidence:
council-batch: stage 1 — 2 parallel units (dispatch concurrently, barrier, then re-run --next):
  utils-test-conftest: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
  utils-typing: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
what_happened: The dispatch plan printed `SubagentType.AI` instead of a resolvable subagent type string (e.g. 'AI Engineer'). Had Claude used council-batch as the driver, it would have had to guess the real Task subagent_type from an enum repr.
root_cause_guess: str(enum_member) used in the formatter instead of .value / the resolved per-feature dev-pick name.

title: Plan drafted wrong facts about existing code despite .context grounding; only the critique panel caught them
evidence: Critic: "Several findings turn on facts the plan got wrong about the existing code ... `list_for_brand` (reads.py:42-48) currently has signature `brand_id, kind, requesting_user_id` — **no `*args`/`conn` param, and it acquires its own connection internally**. ... the byte-identical claim hinges on reproducing that exact whitespace."
what_happened: The spec/plan, written while navigating .context features/conventions/symbols, asserted incorrect signatures and SQL-construction details for the code it planned to modify. The testability critic (reading raw source) corrected them and the plan was revised. The safety net worked, but the index-first drafting produced wrong load-bearing facts — suggesting symbols.json/feature docs lack signature-level fidelity or the skill under-emphasizes verifying against raw source before drafting.
root_cause_guess: The deterministic map (symbols.json) records names/locations but not signatures/string-literal details, and the plan skill encourages drafting from the index without a verify-against-source step.

title: Bare `dummyindex install` (run to probe usage) executed a full destructive install and re-clustered curated .context/ features into community-N
evidence: ⚠️ Important: my `uv run dummyindex install` (which I expected to just print usage) actually **executed a full install** — it installed the skill to user scope, and critically it **rebuilt `.context/` (332 files, 189 indexed)**. ... Confirmed — my `install` invocation triggered a destructive re-cluster: it created `community-0` … `community-18` and rewrote `features/INDEX.json` to point at them, exactly the failure mode in memory ([[full-rebuild-destroys-curated-features]]).
what_happened: While trying to discover install flags (because `--help` is rejected, see separate issue), Claude ran `uv run dummyindex install` with no args expecting a usage printout. Instead it performed a full user-scope install AND a full .context/ rebuild of the cwd repo, creating community-0..community-18 feature dirs and overwriting features/INDEX.json plus 16 tracked backbone files — destroying the curated taxonomy. Recovery was only possible because the git tree happened to be clean: `git checkout -- .context && git clean -fd .context`. Claude logged this as a memory lesson at session end.
root_cause_guess: `install` has destructive defaults: with no args it silently targets cwd, installs to user scope, and runs a full ingest/re-cluster of .context/ with no confirmation, no dry-run, and no protection for an existing curated index. Combined with no `--help` support, probing the command IS running it.

title: CLI subcommands reject --help and use terse mandatory-flag errors, forcing risky no-arg probing
evidence:
=== install --help ===
error: unknown install argument '--help'
[...] === rebuild --help full ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `rebuild`: ['--help']
[...] error: council-batch requires --next
what_happened: Three separate occurrences: `dummyindex install --help`, `dummyindex context rebuild --help`, and bare `dummyindex context council-batch` all errored instead of printing help. To learn install's flags Claude had to run the bare command — which triggered the destructive full install (critical issue above). Help text was otherwise only available via the top-level command list, whose long descriptions get truncated in grep/head pipelines.
root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argument parsing without per-subcommand -h/--help handling; `council-batch` makes `--next` mandatory rather than defaulting to it.

title: `dummyindex` not on PATH — bare invocations fail in repos where it is only a venv dev dependency
evidence: === status === /bin/bash: line 1: dummyindex: command not found
what_happened: Both `which dummyindex` and `dummyindex context preflight .` failed (2 occurrences) because the CLI is a uv dev dependency resolved only via `uv run dummyindex`. SKILL.md and council docs all say bare `dummyindex ...`, so Claude burned turns rediscovering the invocation (and later had to inject 'NO bare dummyindex' warnings into all 24 subagent prompts). The Stop hook guards with `command -v dummyindex >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0`, so in such repos the memory-nudge hook can silently no-op depending on hook PATH.
root_cause_guess: Install supports multiple CLI delivery modes (uv tool, venv dep) but the generated skill docs and hooks assume a global PATH binary; no wrapper/launcher detection is recorded per-repo.

title: build --next-wave keyword matching mis-assigns checklist items to wrong specialists, skills, and hooks
evidence: "index": 2, "text": "`features/brand-hub/lib/illustration-upload.ts` — `uploadIllustration` ...", "agent": "frontend-verify", "subagent_type": "general-purpose", "fallback": false ... "index": 3 ... "agent": "prettier-format", "subagent_type": "general-purpose" ... "index": 4 ... `IllustrationCard.tsx` ... "agent": "frontend-reviewer"  ||  Assistant: "the CLI's keyword matching mis-assigned them ... the CLI mis-matched it to `frontend-reviewer` (a *reviewer*, not an implementer) ... the `general-purpose` fallbacks came from keyword mis-matches to capability specialists."
what_happened: First `dummyindex context build --next-wave --json` call: of 5 Wave-1 items, only 1 was correctly assigned. A design-export prep step was matched to typescript-implementer, two pure TS lib helpers were matched to `frontend-verify` (a generated SKILL) and `prettier-format` (a PostToolUse HOOK) — both un-dispatchable, downgraded to general-purpose with `fallback: false` (misleading metadata) — and a component-implementation item was matched to a reviewer agent. Claude detected the mis-assignments itself and overrode all of them, dispatching typescript-implementer manually. Discussed/repeated at L276 and L316.
root_cause_guess: The wave item→agent matcher is naive keyword matching over the whole equipment manifest (including skills and hooks, not just Task-dispatchable agents), with no role awareness (implementer vs reviewer vs verifier) and a `fallback` flag that doesn't reflect the general-purpose downgrade.

title: CLI subcommands reject --help with an error instead of printing usage
evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `assign-files`: ['--help']
=== ALSO check reconcile-stamp help ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `reconcile-stamp`: ['--help']
what_happened: When the agent needed assign-files/reconcile-stamp usage, `--help` on the subcommand errored. It had to grep the monolithic `dummyindex context --help` (whose grepped output was wrapped/garbled fragments like 'concerns.md. Already-assigned files are') and then run `assign-files` bare to extract the usage from its error message ('error: --feature <feature_id> and at least one --file <path> are required').

title: Installed skill family (0.22.0) diverges from CLI (0.23.0); divergence detected but never remediated
evidence: Skill header: "> **Installed from dummyindex `0.22.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`."  ||  `dummyindex --version` output: "0.23.0"
what_happened: All three dummyindex skills used in the session (plan, build) self-identify as installed from 0.22.0 while the CLI reported 0.23.0. The skill's own instruction says to re-run `dummyindex install --scope user` on divergence, but the agent ran the version check, saw 0.23.0, and proceeded without ever flagging or fixing the skew — so the session drove a 0.23.0 CLI with 0.22.0-era skill instructions (plausibly explaining the missing '— via <tool>' handling and stale reconcile guidance).
root_cause_guess: The version-check instruction is passive prose the model skims past; nothing in the CLI (e.g., a version handshake in --json output or SessionStart hook) enforces or even reports skill/CLI version mismatch.

title: /dummyindex-build invoked three times in a row with three different argument shapes before execution started
evidence: L255: <command-name>/dummyindex-build</command-name>  (no args) → L259: <command-args>.context/proposals/brand-hub-illustrations</command-args> → L267: <command-args>brand-hub-illustrations</command-args>
what_happened: The user issued /dummyindex-build with no args, then with the proposal directory path, then with the bare slug; only the third invocation produced an assistant run (no assistant output exists between the first two). Whether the earlier attempts were user-cancelled or stalled, the retries suggest the expected argument form (slug vs path vs nothing) isn't obvious, and the skill gave no visible feedback on the first two submissions.
root_cause_guess: The build skill documents '<slug>' loosely ('if they didn't name it, list .context/proposals/ and ask') and doesn't normalize path-style arguments to slugs, leaving the invocation contract ambiguous to users.

title: No CLI verb to compute/write per-feature .hash — orchestrator reimplemented the hash algorithm inline; mcp-server's .hash was missing entirely
evidence: Claude wrote .hash files via raw Python: "agg=hashlib.sha256(''.join(sorted(digs)).encode()).hexdigest(); open(f'.context/features/{fid}/.hash','w').write(agg)" for 13 features. Earlier scan: "mcp-server ... .hash=NO" — "`mcp-server` is fully councilled (stages 1-3 complete) but is the lone feature **missing its `.hash` stamp**."
what_happened: The --recouncil cache semantics depend on features/<id>/.hash, but the CLI offers no command to compute or stamp it. After enrichment Claude had to replicate the exact algorithm (sha256 of sorted per-file sha256 hex digests, verified byte-identical in a prior session) in inline Python for all 13 features. The tool itself had also failed to write mcp-server's .hash during its original council run, leaving cache state inconsistent.
root_cause_guess: Hash stamping is done internally during full council runs but was never exposed as a CLI verb for the reconcile/enrichment path, and at least one code path (mcp-server's council) skips writing it.

title: Deterministic flow detection generated bogus flows (flow-011/012) that a council agent had to prune as misdetected
evidence: reconcile --json removed_files: ".context/features/integrations-oauth/flows/flow-011.json ... flow-012.md" — Claude: "the **4 flow files** (`flow-011`, `flow-012` × json/md) the integrations-oauth agent correctly pruned as misdetected."
what_happened: During re-enrichment, the integrations-oauth council agent found two flows in the curated index that did not correspond to real code paths and deleted all four files. The misdetected flows had been sitting in the index serving wrong answers since the original build.
root_cause_guess: The deterministic flow extractor over-detects entry-point chains; nothing validates generated flows against source until a human/agent enrichment pass happens to look.

title: Full `context rebuild` re-clusters the feature layer, orphaning all 14 curated council-authored features
evidence: "`dummyindex context rebuild` (full, no `--changed`) regenerates `map/` + `tree.json` (good — picks up new modules) BUT re-runs graph community-detection from scratch, overwriting `features/INDEX.json` with raw `community-N` clusters and dropping the architect's Phase-2 structural regrouping (`_structural-plan.json` is deleted). The prior curated semantic features ... survive only as **orphaned folders on disk + in git** — they're no longer in INDEX.json." ... away_summary: "I recovered the curated `.context` features after a full rebuild scrambled them"
what_happened: After `rebuild --changed` proved blind to a new 18-file module, the user picked "Full /dummyindex pipeline". The full rebuild wrote 295 files, replaced the 14 curated semantic features in INDEX.json with 17 generic community-0..16 clusters, deleted _structural-plan.json, and also modified curated prose (.context/PROJECT.md, architecture/overview.md, conventions/naming.md). Claude spent most of a 44-minute turn (214 messages) on manual recovery: git checkout of INDEX.json, rm -rf community-*, then hand-rebuilding the feature. Claude wrote a memory note titled full-rebuild-destroys-curated-features.
root_cause_guess: Full rebuild unconditionally re-runs community detection and overwrites features/INDEX.json with raw clusters; it has no merge/preserve path for an existing curated feature roster, no pre-rebuild snapshot, and no warning that the feature layer will be reset.

title: Full rebuild's file discovery is manifest-seeded: silently skips files newer than the prior manifest (3-file extraction gap survives a full rebuild)
evidence: "app/**/*.py with defs>0 but 0 extracted symbols: 3 ... app/core/integrations/credentials.py src_defs=11 in_files.json=False ... tokens.py: ast.parse OK -> dummyindex extraction bug, not bad syntax" ... "it's a **dummyindex `rebuild` bug**: a full rebuild doesn't index files that are newer than the prior manifest (its file discovery is manifest-seeded, not a fresh walk). The committed map was already missing them; my rebuild didn't fix it and `--changed` can't."
what_happened: Even after the destructive FULL rebuild (176 files / 875 symbols), three Python files with real defs (credentials.py 11 defs, tokens.py 1, _token_formatters.py 3) had zero extracted symbols and were absent from files.json. All three parse cleanly with ast.parse and are exactly the three files missing from the pre-rebuild manifest. This later caused two reality-check false positives ("file not found") against accurate docs.
root_cause_guess: rebuild seeds its file walk from the existing cache/manifest.json instead of doing a fresh filesystem walk, so files absent from the prior manifest are never discovered/extracted even on a full rebuild.

title: No status verb: `dummyindex --status` and `dummyindex context status` both nonexistent; Claude had to guess three times
evidence: "error: unknown command '--status'\nUsage: dummyindex <command> [args]" (from `dummyindex --status 2>&1 || dummyindex context status 2>&1 || true`; only `dummyindex context hooks status` eventually worked)
what_happened: After running --refresh, the skill flow led Claude to show drift/hook health. It tried `dummyindex --status`, then `dummyindex context status` — neither exists — before landing on `context hooks status`, which only covers the hook. There is no single command summarizing index health/drift/version, so the orchestrator burns turns guessing.
root_cause_guess: CLI surface lacks a top-level `status` command even though the skill narrative implies status visibility; SKILL.md doesn't enumerate the exact health-check invocations.

title: No CLI verb to add a single feature to a curated index — Claude hand-built feature.json with ad-hoc Python
evidence: "No single community is a clean MCP feature. So \"rename one community\" is dead, and re-clustering everything (full orchestrator) would discard your 14 curated council docs. The right path is the surgical one: **keep the good map update, restore the 14 curated features from git, and add a properly-derived `mcp-server` feature**"
what_happened: To index the new module without losing curated features, the only supported paths were `features-rename` (rename an existing community) or full re-cluster (destructive). Neither worked, so Claude wrote a custom Python heredoc that derived members/entry-points from map/symbols.json and wrote .context/features/mcp-server/feature.json + INDEX.json entries by hand — reverse-engineering the schema from an existing feature folder first. Fragile and unrepeatable.
root_cause_guess: The CLI assumes features only come from clustering; there is no `context feature add --from-path app/core/mcp` style verb for surgically scaffolding a feature on an already-curated index.

title: Community detection fragments a cohesive new package across 6+ clusters, mixing source and tests
evidence: "The clustering is genuinely fragmented — `community-3` has 8 MCP files (but buried among 26 files dominated by `core/ai/*`), `community-6` has 7 (mostly MCP *tests*), the rest scattered 1–2 each. No single community is a clean MCP feature."
what_happened: Even setting aside the destructiveness, the full rebuild's clustering scattered the 18-file app/core/mcp/ package across at least 6 community clusters (community-0/3/6/10/11...), with tests and unrelated core/ai files interleaved, so no cluster could be renamed into the mcp-server feature. This made the rebuild output unusable as a starting point.
root_cause_guess: Graph community detection over the symbol/import graph ignores package/directory boundaries and test-vs-source separation as clustering priors.

title: Proposal shipped with a factually wrong rationale that dummyindex grounding never caught
evidence: My earlier framing — and the **Python port's own module docstring** (`app/core/mcp/tools/brand.py:30`, "the static token tree comes from `design_tokens()`… not the TS Supabase/proxy layer") — implied the TS *MCP* read tokens from Supabase and the port regressed that. **That's wrong.** The TS MCP tools always read the static file; there was never a DB-backed MCP token path.
what_happened: During the /dummyindex-plan run, Claude wrote spec.md/plan.md framing the change as 'restoring' a DB-backed token path the TS app supposedly had (stated explicitly at the investigation stage: 'The TS MCP server read design tokens from Supabase (brand_themes); the Python port replaced that with the static tokens.json'). The 'consistency-checked' propose flow, the .context mcp-server feature docs, and the deterministic scan all failed to flag this; the wrong premise sat in the durable proposal until the USER asked an unrelated follow-up ('how was the main app ... handling brand-theme?'), after which an Explore agent disproved it and Claude had to patch spec.md and add a 'Prior art' correction. Occurred once but the wrong claim persisted across two turns and a written artifact.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex-plan's consistency scan only greps the local .context index — it has no step to verify factual claims inherited from source docstrings or cross-repo history, so the index/proposal happily encodes an inaccurate 'port regression' narrative seeded by a wrong docstring that the original ingest/council also never flagged in concerns.md.

title: Version self-check only detects CLI/skill divergence, not staleness — 0.15.0 wiring passed the check and silently skipped every newer plan feature
evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.15.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`. [...CLI output:] 0.15.0
what_happened: The skill's only freshness guard passed because the repo's CLI and skill were equally old (both 0.15.0). As a result the whole plan run had none of the newer flow: no critique panel, no wave-grouped checklist, no `equip apply --for-proposal`, and crucially no plugin-manager steps (`— via <tool>` task annotation, `equip discover` for capability gaps, install) — directly relevant to the owner's plugin-wiring concern: the session could not exercise plugin discovery/wiring at all, and nothing surfaced that the install was months behind.
root_cause_guess: The installed-version banner compares skill version to local CLI version only; there is no check against the latest release (and no nudge to /dummyindex-update), so equally-stale CLI+skill pairs look healthy forever and per-repo wiring quietly diverges from the shipped product.

title: Consistency scan's related_features are mostly noise for the actual feature
evidence: context propose: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.context/proposals/mcp-design-tokens-from-db (4 files) related features: mcp-server, docs-app-bootstrap, assets-resources, integrations-oauth, artifacts-providers
what_happened: For a 'design tokens from Supabase for the MCP server' proposal, the deterministic scan returned 5 related features of which 3 (docs-app-bootstrap, integrations-oauth, artifacts-providers) are unrelated to design tokens; Claude implicitly confirmed the noise by reading and grounding only on mcp-server and assets-resources. Harmless here but it dilutes the grounding signal that proposal.json/spec.md Consistency blocks carry into build agents.
root_cause_guess: Keyword/symbol-overlap retrieval in `context propose` — the title term 'tokens' likely matched OAuth token handling (integrations-oauth) and shared config/bootstrap symbols, with no relevance ranking or threshold before the hits are written into proposal.json.

title: Bare `dummyindex install --scope user` destructively rebuilt the repo's curated .context/ (re-clustered into 824 community-N dirs) when user only asked to resync skill files
evidence:
.context/        ->  built (3051 files, 1154 indexed, 1699 symbols)
  CLAUDE.md (proj) ->  managed block written … "This is the re-clustering hazard my core memory explicitly warned about: the `install` ran a **full `.context/` rebuild that re-shattered the repo into raw `community-N` clusters**, destroying the curated 13-feature taxonomy and modifying my just-committed `.context/` files plus `.claude/settings.json`." … "the re-cluster created **824 empty `community-N` skeleton dirs**"
what_happened: User said "resync the skill files" after a version question. Claude ran `dummyindex install --scope user`; despite `--scope user`, the install also did a full AST extraction + re-cluster of the cwd repo's .context/, rewriting ~20 tracked files (INDEX.md, PROJECT.md, graph.json, playbooks, meta.json...), creating 824 empty community-N feature dirs, modifying .claude/settings.json (added reconcile-gate hook) and writing a managed CLAUDE.md block — minutes after a carefully hand-merged rebase commit. Recovery took ~15 tool calls (git checkout HEAD -- .context/, rm -rf community-*, revert settings.json). Claude wrote a memory note because this is a known recurring hazard ("core memory explicitly warned about").
root_cause_guess: `install` unconditionally runs a full .context build (fresh-onboarding path) on the cwd repo even when a curated/enriched index exists and even when only user-scope skill files were requested; no detection of an existing curated taxonomy, no --skills-only flag honored by default.

title: Index version stamp stale (0.19.2) while CLI is 0.24.0 — rebuilds and reconcile-stamps don't advance it
evidence:
=== version stamp / schema ===
dummyindex_version: 0.19.2 | schema: 1 | anchor: d53dc9208b7a  … (earlier) === CLI version ===
0.24.0
what_happened: After multiple rebuild --changed runs, refresh-indexes, and a reconcile-stamp under CLI 0.24.0, meta.json still recorded dummyindex_version 0.19.2. Combined with skill files lagging the CLI and memory recording 0.22.0, version diagnosis required cross-checking three layers — this skew is exactly what prompted the user's "what the dummyindex version ?" and the fateful resync.
root_cause_guess: Only full builds (or install) write dummyindex_version into meta.json; incremental update verbs preserve the old stamp, so an actively-maintained index permanently reports the version that first built it.

title: Stale .context index: propose found '(none detected)' related features, and the only documented refresh path is the destructive rebuild
evidence: CLI: "context propose: .../art-direction-paper-rebuild (4 files) related features: (none detected)" — assistant summary: "Related features: the deterministic scan found none — because the `.context/` index predates the as-built art-direction page (none of its components are in `symbols.json`). The real related feature is `brand-hub`; the plan's Wave 1 includes `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` to fix the stale index."
what_happened: The deterministic related-feature retrieval that grounds proposals returned nothing for a page that plainly belongs to the existing brand-hub feature, because symbols.json predated the as-built code. The prescribed remedy — rebuild --changed — is the command that destroys the curated index (issue 1), and reconcile only runs at build close, leaving no safe way to refresh the backbone mid-flight. The second propose (after some commits) did detect brand-hub et al.
root_cause_guess: Index staleness + a catch-22 in the update model: backbone refresh is destructive and curation-preserving reconcile is only wired into the build-close procedure, so plan-time grounding silently degrades on drifted repos.

title: Unrequested auto-commits of .context artifacts — user had to say 'dont commit anything'; the destructive rebuild was committed before inspection
evidence: User: "i need you to make sure this doesnt happen again dont commit anything..." — earlier the assistant had committed the rebuild output and then had to run `git reset --hard HEAD~1` ("Drop the index-shattering rebuild commit")
what_happened: Following dummyindex's 'commit the index as dedicated commits' doctrine, the session auto-committed proposal/equip/index artifacts (~5 commits), including committing the catastrophic rebuild output before checking what it contained, requiring a hard reset. The user then explicitly forbade committing, and all subsequent slices were left uncommitted.
root_cause_guess: Skill guidance to commit .context changes as dedicated commits encourages commit-before-verify and conflicts with this user's no-auto-commit preference; no verification step between rebuild and commit.

title: Proposal scaffold files trip Write-before-Read errors in the flesh-out workflow
evidence: Write to .context/proposals/art-direction-paper-rebuild/plan.md → "<tool_use_error>File has not been read yet. Read it first before writing to it.</tool_use_error>"
what_happened: `dummyindex context propose` scaffolds spec.md/plan.md/checklist.md which the skill then tells Claude to overwrite. The first Write failed because the harness requires reading existing files first; Claude had to Read each scaffold before rewriting (recurred for plan.md here; same pattern hit 4 more times on paper-design notes files).
root_cause_guess: Skill instructions say to flesh out scaffolded files but don't tell Claude to Read them first (or have the CLI emit empty/absent files instead), so every proposal costs extra failed tool calls.

title: dummyindex CLI vanished from ~/.local/bin mid-session, breaking reconcile
evidence:
=== raw reconcile output ===
/bin/bash: line 14: dummyindex: command not found
[...next attempt with full path...]
/bin/bash: line 16: /home/ahmed/.local/bin/dummyindex: No such file or directory
[...earlier piped attempt...]
json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
what_happened: The CLI worked at session start (resolved at /home/ahmed/.local/bin/dummyindex). In the second half of the session every `dummyindex context reconcile` invocation failed — first surfacing as a confusing JSONDecodeError (empty stdout piped to python), then 'command not found', then 'No such file or directory' even with the absolute path. Claude burned several turns doing a filesystem-wide `find /` and finally found a 0.22.0 copy inside the BACKEND repo's venv (/mnt/.../BOS-Mono/backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex) and used that as a workaround. The ~/.local/bin editable 0.19.2 install had been moved to Trash (user reinstalling concurrently); it reappeared as 0.22.0 minutes later. 3+ failed invocations before recovery.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex is a single uv-tool/editable copy at ~/.local/bin with no fallback resolution (no `python -m dummyindex`, no pinned venv shim). A concurrent upgrade/reinstall deletes the launcher for a window, and every skill/hook/doc assumes `dummyindex` on PATH. Backend venv also has the 0.22.0 binary at `backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex`.
what_happened: The session-memory handoff injected at SessionStart records that during the previous session the dummyindex CLI upgrade broke the existing editable install (binary 'trashed', had to be reinstalled), and the upgrade did NOT sync the `.claude/skills/dummyindex/**` skill family — leaving a 0.19.2 skill driving a 0.22.0 CLI across three minor versions. A second stray 0.22.0 binary also ended up in `backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex`. The mismatch persisted into this session (PR #44 stat at L28 shows `.claude/skills/dummyindex/.dummyindex_version` committed at the stale version).

The /dummyindex-update three-layer flow either wasn't used or didn't exist on that version; uv-tool/editable installs are fragile across upgrades and multiple binaries (user PATH vs project venv) can shadow each other.

what_happened: User pasted a failing GitHub Actions pre-commit run (`uv run pre-commit run --all-files --config .pre-commit-config-ci.yaml`). The detect-secrets hook flagged roughly 140 'Hex High Entropy String' locations, virtually all in committed dummyindex output: .context/map/files.json (per-file `"sha256": "a4c3095ab01..."` content hashes) and .context/source-docs/INDEX.json. The assistant verified they were dummyindex's own content hashes, not secrets, then had to exclude the whole .context/ tree from the hook. The hook also looped on '.secrets.baseline is unstaged' until the refreshed baseline was staged.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex embeds high-entropy sha256 content hashes in committed JSON artifacts and neither documents nor wires any detect-secrets exemption (baseline entry, pragma, or exclude) when it installs into a repo that has secret scanning. Since hashes regenerate on every rebuild, baselining is futile — the generator should emit scanner-safe output or the installer should add the exclude itself.

[assistant summary:] "Rather than one-time-fixing files that would regress on the next index rebuild, I added a global `exclude: ^(\.context/|\.claude/skills/dummyindex/)` to both `.pre-commit-config.yaml` and `.pre-commit-config-ci.yaml`."

This downstream workaround had to be invented per-repo by the consuming session.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex's install/wiring step doesn't detect an existing pre-commit/secret-scanning setup and doesn't ship the needed excludes (or scanner-safe output) itself, so every equipped repo with CI lint hits this wall and resolves it ad hoc — losing all linting on the generated trees as collateral.

what_happened: The dummyindex skill family (council phase files, .dummyindex_version) is vendored and committed inside the consumer repo's .claude/skills/dummyindex/, so its formatting violations broke the repo's CI, and a consumer-repo commit ('Add new council phases for reality checks, flow refinement, tree enrichment, and context lookups using dummyIndex') shows the vendored copy being developed/diverged inside the app repo rather than upstream. title: Mid-session CLI upgrade trashed the editable install and left skill files at 0.19.2 while the CLI moved to 0.22.0 (unsynced version skew)
evidence: **CLI upgraded 0.19.2→0.22.0** mid-session (old editable `~/.local/bin/dummyindex` trashed, reinstalled 0.22.0); skill files at `.claude/skills/dummyindex/**` still say 0.19.2 — run `dummyindex install --scope user` to sync if desired (everything worked despite the mismatch).
what_happened: The prior-session handoff (carried into this session at SessionStart) records that upgrading the CLI destroyed the existing editable install at ~/.local/bin/dummyindex mid-session, and that the upgrade did NOT update the repo's skill procedure files, which still claim 0.19.2 three versions behind the 0.22.0 binary. The user is left to remember to run `dummyindex install --scope user` manually; the skew persisted across at least two sessions (it is restated in core-memories.md: 'The skill procedure files under `.claude/skills/dummyindex/**` may still read `0.19.2`').
root_cause_guess: CLI upgrade and skill-file installation are separate, manually-coordinated layers: upgrading the package neither preserves editable installs nor re-runs `dummyindex install` to sync skills/wiring, so Claude can be driven by 0.19.2-era SKILL.md instructions against a 0.22.0 CLI.

title: Global dummyindex directive misfires at BOS-Mono root: .context/ exists but has no HOW_TO_USE.md / INDEX.md / PROJECT.md / features/
evidence: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md line 5: "When working in a repo that has a `.context/` folder at its root, **read `.context/HOW_TO_USE.md` before any non-trivial task** ... Navigate `.context/INDEX.md` → `.context/PROJECT.md` → `.context/features/<id>/` ... flag it and run `dummyindex context rebuild --changed`." — vs live state: "ls: cannot access '.context/HOW_TO_USE.md': No such file or directory" (same for INDEX.md, PROJECT.md, config.json, equipment.json). /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/.context/ contains only docs/ and memory/; root CLAUDE.md line 20: ".context/ ← Docs + plain-markdown agent memory".
what_happened: BOS-Mono root's .context/ is NOT a dummyindex index — it is the project's own pre-existing 'Dev Context Layer' (~100 curated, git-tracked product/research docs under .context/docs/ plus a team memory store under .context/memory/). dummyindex was never installed at the root (no managed CLAUDE.md block, no SessionStart drift hook in .claude/settings.json, no meta.json/config.json) — intentionally, since the repo's docs delegate dummyindex to the frontend/ and backend/ submodules. title: No foreign-.context ownership guard: preflight unconditionally claims `.context/**` as managed
evidence: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/memory/dummyindex/dummyindex/context/domains/preflight/render.py: "_MANAGED_PATHS = (\".context/**\", \".claude/CLAUDE.md  (managed block only — surrounding content preserved)\", \".claude/settings.json  (one additive SessionStart hook)\")" — rendered under "## Will write / manage". No code path in preflight inventories or warns about an existing `.context/` that lacks `meta.json`.
what_happened: Latent hazard made concrete by this repo: if anyone runs `/dummyindex` at BOS-Mono root (plausible, given the global directive in issue 1 points there), preflight would announce it will 'write / manage' `.context/**` — which at this root means the project's curated docs tree and team memory store — with no warning that the directory belongs to another system. Git log confirms it has not fired yet (no dummyindex writes ever landed in the root .context/), but the live state is one invocation away from polluting a tracked docs tree.
root_cause_guess: Preflight inventories .claude/ (settings validity, rules, agents, CLAUDE.md block) and git cleanliness, but never reads `.context/meta.json` to verify ownership before promising to manage the tree. Needs a 'foreign .context detected (no meta.json) — refuse or require --force/alternate dir' check.

title: Mono root memory file is lowercase .claude/claude.md while dummyindex and settings permissions target uppercase .claude/CLAUDE.md
evidence: .claude/ listing shows only "claude.md" (8473 bytes, no CLAUDE.md); .claude/settings.json permissions: "Edit(.claude/CLAUDE.md)", "Write(.claude/CLAUDE.md)"; dummyindex preflight render.py managed path: ".claude/CLAUDE.md  (managed block only — surrounding content preserved)".
what_happened: On this case-sensitive Linux filesystem the mono's per-directory guide is `.claude/claude.md` (lowercase). The repo's own settings.json permission allow-rules reference the uppercase path that doesn't exist, and dummyindex's managed-block writer hard-codes `.claude/CLAUDE.md` — so a root dummyindex run would create a second, parallel memory file beside the human-authored lowercase one instead of appending the managed block to it. Depending on Claude Code's case handling, the lowercase file may also not load as project memory at all, silencing the repo's 'thin mono-scope guide'.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex's bootstrap writer does an exact-case path check for `.claude/CLAUDE.md` and never does a case-insensitive lookup for an existing claude.md before creating its own file.

title: Help text drifted two releases behind the CLI surface: no `equip discover`/`equip install`, no `build --next-wave`
evidence: cli/help.py:299: `build --proposal S (--next | --check "<item>" | --status) [--json]` — no --next-wave; yet cli/build_loop/dispatch.py:78 `want_wave = "--next-wave" in rest` and its error msg lists `--next, --next-wave, ...`. `dummyindex context --help | grep -E 'discover|equip install'` matches nothing except 'are discovered automatically', while cli/equip/dispatch.py routes `EquipVerb.DISCOVER` (line 96) and `EquipVerb.INSTALL` (line 98) and the equip skill documents `dummyindex context equip discover ...` / `equip install <plugin>@<marketplace> [--yes] [--scope project|local|user]`.

title: Per-subcommand --help is rejected as an unknown argument instead of printing usage
evidence: `dummyindex context equip --help` -> `error: unknown argument(s) for `equip`: ['--help']`; identical failures for `context build --help`, `context reconcile --help`, `context reconcile-stamp --help`, `context council-batch --help`.
what_happened: Only `dummyindex --help` and `dummyindex context --help` work; the conventional `<subcommand> --help` errors out (exit non-zero). Skills instruct agents to run these subcommands, and the natural flag-discovery reflex fails, pushing agents to guess flags or grep source instead.
root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled arg parsing in each verb handler treats --help as an unexpected token; no shared fallthrough that prints the relevant help.py section.

title: Stale packaging artifacts in repo: dummyindex.egg-info pinned at 0.21.0 and an old build/lib tree
evidence: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/memory/dummyindex/dummyindex.egg-info/PKG-INFO: `Name: dummyindex` / `Version: 0.21.0` vs pyproject.toml `version = "0.25.0"`. Stale `build/bdist.linux-x86_64` + `build/lib/dummyindex/` tree also present. Both are gitignored (`.gitignore:6 *.egg-info/`, `.gitignore:9 /build/`).
what_happened: Leftover setuptools artifacts from a 0.21.0-era build sit at the repo root, four releases stale. Harmless to git, but the egg-info advertises the wrong version to any setuptools/pkg_resources-style introspection run inside the repo, its SOURCES.txt no longer reflects the package, and a stale build/lib is the classic setuptools trap where deleted/renamed files get re-bundled into the next locally built wheel/sdist unless cleaned. Entry points themselves are consistent everywhere (pyproject [project.scripts], egg-info and dist-info all say `dummyindex = dummyindex.__main__:main`).
root_cause_guess: A local `pip install .` / `setup.py`-style build at 0.21.0 left artifacts; no `rm -rf build *.egg-info` hygiene step in the release/install flow. Fix: delete both directories.

title: Install-method drift: CLI is now a pip --user copy, not the recorded uv-tool install; broken `dummyindexy` uv receipt lingers
evidence: `which dummyindex` -> /home/ahmed/.local/bin/dummyindex with shebang `#!/usr/bin/python3.11`; dist-info INSTALLER file contains `pip`; `uv tool list | grep -i dummy` -> empty, but stderr warns: `warning: Tool `dummyindexy` environment not found (run `uv tool install dummyindexy --reinstall` to reinstall)`. ~/.local/share/uv/tools/ contains only kimi-cli. From a neutral cwd, import resolves to the site-packages copy; from the repo root, `python3.11 -c "import dummyindex"` resolves to the repo tree (cwd shadowing).
what_happened: The persisted gotcha ('uv-tool COPY; reinstall via uv tool install --force --editable <repo>') no longer matches reality: dummyindex is installed via pip --user into system python3.11 site-packages. Following the recorded reinstall advice would create a second, uv-managed install overwriting ~/.local/bin/dummyindex and could leave two divergent copies. A dead uv receipt for `dummyindexy` (the project's old name) emits warnings on every `uv tool list`. The cwd-shadowing gotcha (repo-root python masks installed-copy staleness) was re-confirmed live.
root_cause_guess: Install method changed at the 00:36 Jun-13 reinstall (pip instead of uv tool) without updating the recorded gotcha or cleaning the orphaned `dummyindexy` uv receipt (`uv tool uninstall dummyindexy` or delete its receipt).
